take_off: The flight phase begins with an Airbus A320 positioned on the ground at latitude 49.00551 and longitude 2.55497, near Paris Charles de Gaulle Airport. At the very start of the flight, the aircraft is stationary, with a ground speed of just 12 knots and no vertical speed, as it prepares for departure. The heading is set to 264°, indicating alignment with the runway for a westward takeoff.     Approximately 3.9 minutes after departure, the aircraft has moved slightly to latitude 49.01826 and longitude 2.5513, maintaining a ground speed of 11 knots and still at an altitude of 0 feet. The heading shifts to 351°, suggesting a repositioning maneuver as the aircraft completes its taxi and prepares for the initial climb phase.    By 12.1 minutes into the flight, the A320 has taken off and climbed significantly, reaching an altitude of 4,675 feet. Its position is now at latitude 49.03119 and longitude 2.7137, heading eastward with a track of 85°. The ground speed has increased dramatically to 233 knots, and the vertical speed is a robust 2,176 feet per minute, indicating a strong climb rate as the aircraft ascends over the Île-de-France region, leaving the urban sprawl of Paris behind.    Four minutes later, at 16.1 minutes into the flight, the aircraft has climbed further to an altitude of 15,025 feet. Its position has shifted southward to latitude 48.70267 and longitude 2.70488, with a new heading of 196°. The ground speed has accelerated to 390 knots, and the vertical speed has increased to 2,752 feet per minute, reflecting a steep climb as the A320 transitions over the rolling countryside of central France.    At 20.1 minutes into the flight, the A320 reaches an altitude of 23,550 feet, continuing its climb at a reduced vertical speed of 1,344 feet per minute. The aircraft is now at latitude 48.24669 and longitude 2.73574, heading further south with a track of 170°. The ground speed has increased to 442 knots as the flight progresses over the Loire Valley, a region known for its vineyards and historic châteaux.    By 24.1 minutes into the flight, the aircraft has climbed to 29,500 feet, nearing its cruising altitude. Its position is now at latitude 47.76069 and longitude 2.98201, maintaining a southerly heading of 157°. The ground speed has increased to 484 knots, while the vertical speed has decreased to 960 feet per minute, indicating a more gradual climb as the A320 crosses over the Burgundy region, renowned for its picturesque landscapes and world-class wines.    At 28.1 minutes into the flight, the A320 reaches an altitude of 34,825 feet, continuing its southward trajectory at latitude 47.25343 and longitude 3.29803. The heading remains steady at 157°, and the ground speed has slightly increased to 492 knots. The vertical speed is now 896 feet per minute, as the aircraft nears its cruising altitude while flying over the Auvergne region, characterized by its volcanic terrain and lush greenery.    Finally, at 32.1 minutes into the flight, the Airbus A320 levels off at its cruising altitude of 37,000 feet. The aircraft is now positioned at latitude 46.93569 and longitude 3.86895, heading southeast with a track of 121°. The ground speed has decreased slightly to 458 knots, and the vertical speed has stabilized at 0 feet per minute, marking the transition to the cruise phase. The flight now soars over the Massif Central, a mountainous region in southern France, as it continues its journey toward its destination.
cruising: The aircraft, an Airbus A320, begins its cruising phase at an altitude of 37,000 feet, maintaining a steady course over central France. At the start of this phase, precisely 0 minutes into the flight, the aircraft is positioned at latitude 46.93569 and longitude 3.86895, heading southeast with a track of 121°. The ground speed is a consistent 458 knots, and the vertical speed is zero, indicating a stable altitude. The flight is passing over the rolling countryside of the Burgundy region, known for its vineyards and historic towns. The squawk code is set to '1000', ensuring proper identification by air traffic control.  Four minutes later, the aircraft continues its southeast trajectory, now at latitude 46.66562 and longitude 4.49401. The track adjusts slightly to 122°, and the altitude dips marginally to 36,975 feet, though the ground speed remains steady at 458 knots. The vertical speed indicates no significant climb or descent, and the aircraft glides smoothly over the Saône-et-Loire department, a region dotted with charming villages and the winding Saône River.  By the eighth minute of the flight, the A320 reaches latitude 46.39151 and longitude 5.11474, maintaining its track of 122°. The altitude returns to 37,000 feet, with a slight vertical speed of 64 feet per minute, suggesting a gentle correction in altitude. The ground speed remains unchanged at 458 knots. Below, the Jura Mountains come into view, their forested slopes and limestone ridges offering a striking contrast to the plains left behind.  At 12 minutes into the flight, the aircraft adjusts its heading slightly to 127° as it crosses into the Rhône-Alpes region, near latitude 46.11353 and longitude 5.72752. The altitude increases slightly to 37,025 feet, though the vertical speed of -64 feet per minute suggests a minor stabilization. The ground speed increases to 461 knots, and the A320 glides over the Ain department, with the Alps beginning to rise in the distance.  By the 16th minute, the aircraft reaches latitude 45.75131 and longitude 6.25568, tracking 135° and cruising at 37,000 feet. The ground speed increases further to 476 knots, and the vertical speed stabilizes at zero. The flight now passes over the Haute-Savoie region, with the majestic Mont Blanc massif visible to the east, its snow-capped peaks glistening in the sunlight.  At the 20-minute mark, the A320 continues southeast, now at latitude 45.37987 and longitude 6.79337, maintaining a track of 134°. The altitude rises slightly to 37,025 feet, with a vertical speed of 64 feet per minute. The ground speed climbs to 484 knots as the aircraft crosses into the Savoie region, with the dramatic alpine landscape stretching out below.  Four minutes later, at 24 minutes into the flight, the aircraft maintains its track of 134° and altitude of 37,025 feet, though the vertical speed briefly dips to -64 feet per minute. Positioned at latitude 45.00177 and longitude 7.33746, the A320 flies over the Piedmont region of northern Italy, with the city of Turin visible in the distance, nestled at the foot of the Alps. The ground speed decreases slightly to 471 knots.  By the 28th minute, the aircraft reaches latitude 44.63708 and longitude 7.85221, tracking 135° and cruising at 37,000 feet. The ground speed increases again to 478 knots, and the vertical speed stabilizes at zero. The flight now traverses the rolling hills and fertile plains of southern Piedmont, with the Ligurian coast beginning to appear on the horizon.  At 32 minutes into the flight, the A320 maintains its track of 135° and altitude of 37,025 feet, with a ground speed of 482 knots and no vertical speed. Positioned at latitude 44.25696 and longitude 8.37832, the aircraft approaches the Ligurian coastline, with the sparkling waters of the Mediterranean Sea coming into view.  Four minutes later, at 36 minutes into the flight, the aircraft continues along its southeast trajectory, now at latitude 43.87381 and longitude 8.89893. The track remains 135°, and the altitude holds steady at 37,025 feet, with a ground speed of 480 knots. The A320 is now flying parallel to the Italian Riviera, with the picturesque towns of Liguria dotting the coastline below.  Finally, at 40 minutes into the flight, the aircraft begins a gradual descent, indicated by a vertical speed of -960 feet per minute. The altitude decreases to 35,700 feet as the A320 reaches latitude 43.4939 and longitude 9.40554, tracking 136° with a ground speed of 478 knots. The flight is now approaching the Tuscan coast, with the Tyrrhenian Sea stretching out to the south and the rolling hills of Tuscany visible inland. This marks the end of the cruising phase as the aircraft prepares for its descent toward its destination.
landing: The aircraft, an Airbus A320, begins its descent at cruising altitude, 35,700 feet, over the Ligurian coastline, near the city of La Spezia, Italy. It is tracking southeast at a heading of 136° and maintaining a ground speed of 478 knots. The vertical speed indicates a steady descent at -960 feet per minute as the aircraft transitions into its landing phase. The time since the start of this phase is marked at 0 minutes. Below, the rugged coastline and the shimmering waters of the Ligurian Sea stretch out, offering a scenic view to passengers.   Four minutes into the descent, the aircraft has reached an altitude of 32,675 feet, continuing on the same heading of 136°. The ground speed has decreased slightly to 458 knots, and the descent rate has increased to -1,472 feet per minute. The A320 is now approaching the Tuscan Archipelago, with the island of Elba visible to the east. The descent remains smooth and controlled as the aircraft progresses southeastward.  By the eighth minute, the aircraft has altered its heading slightly to 108°, now descending through 26,525 feet. The ground speed has reduced further to 404 knots, and the vertical speed remains consistent at -1,408 feet per minute. The A320 is now flying over the Tyrrhenian Sea, with the coastline of Tuscany visible in the distance. The descent continues to be steady as the aircraft prepares for its approach.  At 12 minutes into the phase, the aircraft adjusts its heading to 119° and descends to 21,150 feet. The ground speed remains stable at 405 knots, while the descent rate eases slightly to -896 feet per minute. The A320 is now approaching the Lazio region, with the rolling hills and historic towns of central Italy coming into view. The descent is smooth, and the aircraft maintains its trajectory toward its destination.  Sixteen minutes into the flight phase, the aircraft has descended to 17,325 feet, maintaining a heading of 120°. The ground speed has decreased to 328 knots, and the vertical speed remains steady at -896 feet per minute. The A320 is now flying over the Lazio countryside, with the city of Civitavecchia and its port visible below. The descent continues as the aircraft aligns itself for the approach.  At the 20-minute mark, the aircraft is at 14,125 feet, still on a heading of 120°. The ground speed has increased slightly to 331 knots, and the descent rate has increased to -1,024 feet per minute. The A320 is now nearing the outskirts of Rome, with the Tiber River winding its way through the landscape below. The approach becomes more defined as the aircraft prepares for its final descent.  By the 24th minute, the aircraft has descended to 9,675 feet and adjusted its heading to 141°. The ground speed has decreased to 280 knots, and the descent rate remains steady at -1,024 feet per minute. The A320 is now over the Roman countryside, with the sprawling city of Rome visible in the distance. The descent is smooth, and the aircraft is on track for its final approach.  At 28 minutes into the phase, the aircraft has descended to 6,200 feet and shifted its heading to 162°. The ground speed has decreased to 220 knots, and the vertical speed briefly transitions to a slight climb at 128 feet per minute, indicating a minor adjustment in the approach path. The A320 is now directly approaching Rome, with landmarks such as the Vatican and the Colosseum visible to passengers on the left side of the aircraft.  By the 32nd minute, the aircraft has descended further to 2,850 feet and altered its heading to 234°. The ground speed has increased slightly to 224 knots, and the descent rate has resumed at -896 feet per minute. The A320 is now in the final stages of its approach, with the runways of Rome Fiumicino Airport coming into view. The descent is precise, and the aircraft is well-aligned for landing.  At 36 minutes, the aircraft is at 2,475 feet, tracking north at a heading of 342°. The ground speed has decreased to 170 knots, and the descent rate is -832 feet per minute. The A320 is now on its final approach, with the runway lights of Fiumicino Airport guiding the way. The descent is smooth, and the landing gear is likely deployed at this stage.  By the 40th minute, the aircraft has touched down, coming to a halt at an altitude of 0 feet. The ground speed has reduced to 38 knots, and the heading has shifted to 326°. The A320 is now taxiing on the runway, completing its landing phase. The passengers are greeted by the sights of Rome Fiumicino Airport as the aircraft makes its way to the terminal.  Over the next several minutes, the aircraft continues taxiing at reduced speeds, with minor heading adjustments as it navigates the taxiways. By the 48th minute, the A320 has come to a complete stop at the gate, marking the end of its journey. The engines are powered down, and the passengers prepare to disembark, concluding a smooth and professional landing phase.
